Examined Argon2id API requirements and defensive validation strategies
Examined Argon2id API requirements and defensive validation strategies

Verifying Konscious Argon2 base class API (properties, GetBytes)
Looking at the Konscious API for Argon2id hashing, I need to understand the constructor requirements and available properties like Salt, Iterations, MemorySize, and DegreeOfParallelism. The key consideration is whether to use the synchronous GetBytes method or the async variant—since GetBytes internally uses Task.Run with .Result, I should probably reach for GetBytesAsync to avoid blocking in a request context, though blocking inside the semaphore might be acceptable depending on the architecture.

One critical detail: the Argon2id constructor throws if the password is empty or null, and there are validation thresholds for other parameters (iterations, memory size, degree of parallelism all need to be at least 1, and the output byte count can't exceed 1024). Since Identity's PasswordHasher validates minimum password length before calling the hasher, an empty password shouldn't reach the hashing logic in normal flow, but I should still guard defensively—returning Failed in VerifyHashedPassword if an empty password is provided, rather than letting it throw.

For the Hash method, I'll throw an ArgumentException if the password is empty to match Konscious's behavior and keep the contract clear. Let me verify the Argon2id subclass file exists.

Show less
Done
Konscious API confirmed (Argon2id(byte[]), Salt, Iterations, MemorySize in KiB, DegreeOfParallelism, GetBytesAsync). Let me confirm the Argon2id subclass and a valid package version.

The API is stable across versions 1.2 and 1.3, so this should work reliably. Now I need to verify the exact .NET Identity interface signatures I'm working with.

Konscious has no git tags (NuGet-only); I'll pin the known-stable 1.3.1 and note it. Now let me verify the exact .NET 10 Identity store interface signatures I'll implement, so the method shapes match precisely.

Verifying lockout, role, email, phone stores + password hasher + verification enum
I've confirmed the IPasswordHasher<TUser> interface with HashPassword returning a string and VerifyHashedPassword for validation, plus the PasswordVerificationResult enum values. Now checking if .NET 10 includes the IUserConfirmation interface.

For AddIdentityCore setup, I need to configure IdentityOptions carefully: password requirements (minimum 12 characters, no complexity rules), lockout policy (5 attempts in 5 minutes), and username character restrictions. Since the spec allows usernames with any characters in the 3-64 range, I'm working through how to configure Identity's AllowedUserNameCharacters to avoid rejecting valid usernames while still respecting the length constraints. I could map both unique constraint violations and CHECK violations to failed IdentityResult with appropriate messages, or add a custom IUserValidator to enforce the 3-64 length requirement upfront. For now I'll rely on the DB CHECK plus error mapping so that invalid usernames fail gracefully and are testable, with a dedicated validator as a nice-to-have addition later.

The flow is clear: UserManager.CreateAsync runs validators first (which won't catch a 2-char name since the default validator doesn't check length), then hashes the password, then calls my store's CreateAsync. When the store inserts and the DB CHECK fails, Npgsql throws a 23514 exception that I catch and convert to a failed IdentityResult. Now I need to write out all the files for this implementation. asher tests without a container, then integration tests for the user store with a container, and a small test double for the clock. I'm also updating the project files to add the Argon2id package reference and framework dependencies, then modifying Program.cs to wire up the identity services.

I need to verify that RestaurantMetrics has the password hashing duration histogram so I can pass the right callback to the identity setup.

Architected dependency injection pattern for singleton callback integration
Architected dependency injection pattern for singleton callback integration

Reading RestaurantMetrics to see the hash-duration instrument
The hasher accepts a callback for recording password hash duration metrics, which gets wired up in Program.cs to call the RestaurantMetrics singleton method. Since both the hasher and metrics are singletons, I need to use a factory pattern when registering the hasher to resolve RestaurantMetrics and pass its recording method as the callback.

I'll create an AddRestaurantIdentity extension method in the WebApplication layer that handles this DI setup cleanly, then write out the full implementation files to verify everything parses correctly.

Architected comprehensive Identity store with Dapper integration and Argon2 hashing
Architected comprehensive Identity store with Dapper integration and Argon2 hashing

Checking for dotnet SDK and preparing working dirs
I'll write out the C# entity classes now, starting with the Person model that includes properties for identity, username, and display information.

Now I'm building the DapperUserStore class that implements multiple Identity interfaces to handle user management, password storage, security stamps, lockout logic, two-factor authentication, authenticator keys, recovery codes, roles, email, and phone number functionality. It'll depend on a database connection factory, clock service, identifier factory, and data protection provider for encrypting sensitive data.

I'm setting up the data protection purpose string for TOTP secrets and mapping the database columns to the Person entity properties, deciding whether to use Dapper's auto-mapping with snake_case configuration or write an explicit mapping function.

I'll go with explicit column aliases in the SELECT statement — it's verbose but self-contained and doesn't require global Dapper configuration that might conflict with other parts of the codebase. I'm defining a constant string that lists all the person table columns with their PascalCase aliases.

Defining the column mapping constant... Dapper matches column names to properties case-insensitively, but underscores aren't automatically ignored unless I enable MatchNamesWithUnderscores. Since Postgres lowercases unquoted identifiers, aliasing to PersonIdentifier creates a column named personidentifier, which Dapper then matches to the PersonIdentifier property case-insensitively.

For the type mappings, Dapper and Npgsql handle the conversions automatically: uuid columns map to Guid properties, and citext maps to string. When inserting parameters like @Username into a citext column, Npgsql sends it as text and Postgres applies the implicit cast from text to citext. For WHERE clauses comparing a citext column to a text parameter, Postgres resolves the = operator by implicitly casting the text parameter to citext, so the comparison works seamlessly. To avoid any potential operator resolution ambiguity, I'll add an explicit cast to citext on the parameter side. Whether Npgsql sends the string as text or unknown type, casting the parameter to citext ensures the comparison uses the correct operator. For inserts into citext columns, the implicit text-to-citext coercion should work fine, but I might cast there too for consistency. Casting to citext is the safest approach since it works regardless of how Npgsql sends the parameter type. I'll apply the same pattern to the email lookup query, then move on to writing the INSERT statement for creating new records. For UpdateAsync, I'm updating all mutable columns where the person identifier matches, but I'm skipping an optimistic concurrency check since our schema doesn't have a separate concurrency stamp column — just security_stamp which only changes on credential updates. Since the base Identity store doesn't require concurrency handling, the update proceeds unconditionally.

For DeleteAsync, the spec explicitly forbids deletion (persons are deactivated, never deleted, and history must preserve its actors), so I'm throwing NotSupportedException to be honest about what the store supports. Tests can verify this behavior. Now I'm moving on to the lock

-related methods — GetLockoutEndDateAsync, SetLockoutEndDateAsync, IncrementAccessFailedCountAsync, ResetAccessFailedCountAsync, GetAccessFailedCountAsync, GetLockoutEnabledAsync, and SetLockoutEnabledAsync — which all manipulate the user entity's lockout and failed access count properties, and UserManager will persist those changes via UpdateAsync. Two-factor authentication methods are next.

For GetTwoFactorEnabledAsync, I'm checking if the user has a TOTP secret stored, since enrollment means the secret is present. SetTwoFactorEnabledAsync is more complex — Identity expects to toggle 2FA via this method, but our model derives 2FA-enabled status from whether a TOTP secret exists, so there's no independent flag to set. If Identity tries to enable 2FA without an actual secret, that creates an inconsistency, though in practice our enrollment flow sets the authenticator key first and then Identity calls SetTwoFactorEnabledAsync.

I'll implement SetTwoFactorEnabledAsync to clear the secret when disabling (which aligns with removing TOTP), and when enabling, it's a no-op if a secret already exists — trying to enable without a secret won't stick anyway since GetTwoFactorEnabled derives from secret presence, which is the correct behavior. This could confuse Identity if it sets true and reads back false, but our actual flows never call SetTwoFactorEnabledAsync directly. Defining the authenticator key storage and recovery code management... Writing the query logic...

For counting unused recovery codes, I'm querying the table filtered by person identifier and checking for null used_at timestamps. The recovery codes are hashed using SHA256 which produces a 32-byte value, and Npgsql will correctly map the byte array parameter to the bytea column type. I notice the recovery code hash column doesn't have an explicit length constraint like the device secret does, which is worth noting.

Now looking at the roles implementation — I need to query the person_role table to fetch role names for a user, and check role membership using EXISTS queries. There's a potential mismatch here: Identity normalizes role names to uppercase when calling IsInRoleAsync, but our stored role names are lowercase and constrained that way in the database. So when Identity passes "ADMINISTRATOR" to check membership, I need to handle the case conversion properly since our actual stored value is "administrator". The role claim comparison is case-sensitive by default, so if GetRolesAsync returns lowercase role names, they'll match against lowercase role requirements in policies. This is consistent with the normalization I'm doing — the store methods receive uppercase input from Identity and I lowercase it for comparison, then return the lowercase values for claims. For AddToRoleAsync, I'm throwing NotSupported since role grants require a dedicated grantor service. RemoveFromRoleAsync also needs to throw NotSupported since revocation requires recording who performed it as an audit event, which means it must go through the admin service. For GetUsersInRoleAsync, I can implement a straightforward query that joins person and person_role tables filtered by role name and returns the matching persons — it's a read operation and worth including. Now I'm defining the IUserRoleStore interface with these methods.

For the email store, I need to handle SetEmailAsync and GetEmailAsync straightforwardly by reading/writing the EmailAddress field. GetEmailConfirmedAsync is trickier since the schema doesn't have an email_confirmed column and we don't enforce email confirmation for sign-in, so I'll return true to avoid blocking authentication flows. For email confirmation, I'll return true to avoid accidental gating since the feature isn't modeled anyway. For finding users by email, I'm querying the citext column for case-insensitive matching and returning the first result or null. The email normalization is handled at the database level, so the getter just returns the address as-is and the setter is a no-op. Similarly for phone confirmation, I'm treating it as always confirmed and making the setter inert.

For passwords, I'm storing and retrieving the hash directly from the user object, with a helper to check if one exists. For the security stamp, I'm regenerating it as a fresh GUID rather than using Identity's base32 string format, though I need to verify this doesn't cause issues within the UpdateSecurityStamp flow.

Looking at the SecurityStampValidator, it reads the stamp at sign-in and again during validation—both operations go through my getter which returns the stored GUID as a string, so they're consistent. When a credential changes and UpdateSecurityStampAsync is called, it generates a new GUID, which invalidates the old stamp stored in the cookie, forcing a new session. I need to add defensive guards in the CreateAsync method to handle cases where someone bypasses UserManager and calls the store directly—ensuring SecurityStamp gets a new guid if it's empty, PersonIdentifier gets generated if missing, and CreatedAt gets set if it's the default value.

For the user store methods, I'm implementing GetUserId and GetUserName to return the PersonIdentifier and Username respectively, SetUserName to update the username (defaulting to empty string if null), and GetNormalizedUserName to just return the username since the database handles case-insensitivity via citext. FindById parses the guid and queries by PersonIdentifier, while FindByName queries case-insensitively using citext. SetNormalizedUserName is a no-op, and Dispose doesn't need to do anything since connections are managed elsewhere.

For database operations, each method opens a connection through the factory, executes the query, and disposes it immediately. For operations like ReplaceCodesAsync that need atomicity, I'll wrap the delete and insert in a transaction on a single connection. I need to be careful with Dapper's async methods, using CommandDefinition with the cancellation token properly passed through.

For IdentityResult handling, CreateAsync should catch the unique constraint violation and return a failed result with a DuplicateUserName error, while check constraint violations get a format error. UpdateAsync checks if any rows were affected—if zero, it's a concurrency failure or the user wasn't found; if the update tries to set a duplicate username, that's also a constraint violation to handle.

DeleteAsync will throw NotSupportedException since we're not supporting deletions.

I'm using PostgresException from Npgsql to catch specific error codes—UniqueViolation (23505) and CheckViolation (23514)—via the PostgresErrorCodes constants, which requires adding using Npgsql;.

Now I'm implementing the Argon2id password hasher...

Writing hasher implementation... Writing the hashing implementation... Computing the Argon2 hash... The synchronous blocking on the semaphore is acceptable here since Argon2 is CPU-bound anyway, and with a concurrency limit of 4, it won't exhaust the thread pool even under high load.

I'll keep Argon2HashingOptions in the same file as the hasher since they're tightly coupled. Now I'm setting up the DI helper in WebApplication...

Writing DI registration code... Configuring password and sign-in policies... Completing the Argon2id hasher configuration...

The AddDefaultTokenProviders() method pulls in token providers from the Identity Core package—DataProtectorTokenProvider, PhoneNumberTokenProvider, EmailTokenProvider, and AuthenticatorTokenProvider. Since DataProtection is already registered in Program.cs, that dependency is satisfied. AuthenticatorTokenProvider is what handles TOTP verification later, so including it now sets everything up properly even if we're not using all the providers immediately.

I'm also checking whether setting AllowedUserNameCharacters to an empty string actually removes restrictions in Identity's UserValidator, since that's what we're relying on for the username validation logic.

Now I need to verify that services.Replace with a ServiceDescriptor.Singleton factory overload is the right approach for swapping in the custom password hasher. So AddIdentityCore uses TryAddScoped to register the default IPasswordHasher<TUser>, which means there's exactly one registration. When I call Replace, it removes that scoped registration and swaps in my singleton version instead—mixing lifetimes is fine since the resolved service is my singleton. For the Program.cs modification, I need to add the using statement for the Identity namespace and call AddRestaurantIdentity in the services configuration, placing it after AddDataProtection since the store needs the IDataProtectionProvider for token operations. All the dependencies for DapperUserStore check out: the identifier factory, clock, database connection factory, and data protection provider are all registered as singletons, and IdentityErrorDescriber gets registered as scoped by AddIdentityCore. I'm considering making IdentityErrorDescriber optional with a default fallback in the store constructor.

For TOTP protection, the store will create an IDataProtector from the injected IDataProtectionProvider using a purpose string specific to TOTP secrets. Now I need to update the project file to add the necessary framework reference. Adding Argon2 to Directory.Packages.props in the Data access group, then adding the ASP.NET Core framework reference to the test project as well since framework references aren't always transitive at compile time — the test project won't need Argon2 itself since it accesses the hasher through the DataAccess reference. I'll go with EphemeralDataProtectionProvider since it's simpler and doesn't require file system setup — just in-memory keys. Now I'm sketching out the test cases for the password hasher: one to verify that hashing and then verifying a correct password succeeds, another to confirm that verifying with a wrong password fails, and a third to ensure the hash output follows the proper PHC format.

I also need to test that when verifying a hash created with older parameters against a hasher configured with new parameters, it returns SuccessRehashNeeded, plus cases for garbage or empty hashes returning Failed, and confirming that two hashes of the same password produce different results due to random salting. I'm keeping the test parameters lean — memory at 19456 KiB, iterations at 2, parallelism at 1 — which should keep each hash around 15-40ms, making the full test suite run quickly.

For the integration tests with DapperUserStore, I'll use the existing PostgreSqlFixture to manage a shared container and database across the test class. Since tests share the same database, I'll generate a unique username for each test using a Guid suffix to avoid collisions rather than truncating between tests.

For role-related tests, I'll insert person_role rows directly via raw SQL to set up the test data, then verify the read operations work correctly — this tests the actual read path through the store. I'm building a helper method that instantiates DapperUserStore with all its real dependencies (the Npgsql connection factory, a fixed clock, UUID generator, data protection provider, and error describer), and I'll run the schema migrations once during the fixture's initialization.

Actually, running migrations at the start of each test is simpler and still fast since they're idempotent — the second run is a no-op. I'll create an EnsureMigrated helper that runs the migration runner, keeping it consistent with how the existing SchemaMigrationRunnerTests are structured.

Wait, I need to clarify the fixture setup. Each test class gets its own PostgreSqlFixture instance when using IClassFixture<T>, so SchemaMigrationRunnerTests and DapperUserStoreTests will each have their own container — they're isolated from each other, which is fine and simpler than trying to share.

Now for the test cases: I'll write tests that create a person and verify it can be found by ID with all fields intact, check that name lookups are case-insensitive, ensure duplicate usernames return a Failed result with the appropriate error code, validate that usernames shorter than the minimum length trigger a database constraint violation, and confirm that password hashes can be set, updated, and retrieved correctly.

Then I'm testing the security stamp regeneration to ensure it changes and remains a valid GUID, verifying that lockout counters and expiration dates persist through updates and can be reset to zero, and checking that two-factor authentication derives from the authenticator key—where setting the key enables two-factor, retrieving it returns the same decrypted value, and the stored column contains the encrypted version rather than plaintext.

I'm also validating recovery codes by replacing them, confirming the count, redeeming valid codes decrements the count, attempting to reuse or redeem nonexistent codes fails, and verifying codes are stored hashed rather than in plaintext. Finally, I'm testing role reads by inserting a person and directly adding role assignments to check they're reflected correctly.

Continuing with role operations: checking that role names are normalized to uppercase, verifying membership queries work correctly, and confirming users can be retrieved by role. I'm also asserting that AddToRoleAsync and DeleteAsync throw NotSupportedException since role changes go through the admin service. For email lookups, I'm testing case-insensitive matching by setting an email with mixed case and querying with a different case variation.

Now I'm moving on to set up a fixed clock test double in the test project. ect code from the generated set, so the hashing and comparison should work correctly. Recovery codes are case-sensitive and already uppercase, so there's no normalization issue to worry about.

I'm also verifying the email confirmation logic — the person table doesn't have explicit email_confirmed or phone_confirmed columns, but GetEmailConfirmedAsync returns true, which is fine since the email address itself being set indicates confirmation. For username normalization, GetNormalizedUserNameAsync returns the username as-is, and since the database uses case-insensitive text comparison (citext), this aligns with how Identity's FindByName works.

The schema uses username citext UNIQUE, so duplicate usernames with different cases get rejected at the database level with a uniqueness violation, which maps correctly to a DuplicateUserName error. Now I'm ready to write out all the implementation files, starting with the Person entity class.

The store implementation covers the full non-passkey family (user password, security stamp, lockout, two-factor, authenticator key, recovery codes, roles, email, phone), plus the DI setup with Argon2 hasher integration and necessary framework references. Next I'm adding the metrics pipeline for sign-ins, the obligations middleware for forced password changes and TOTP enrollment, TOTP setup with QR code generation and recovery codes (checking the RFC 6238 skew window), the first-admin bootstrap under advisory locks, and the account administration service for staff management, role grants, credential resets, and security event tracking.

I'm also documenting the implementation details: security stamps stored as UUIDs, two-factor state derived from the TOTP secret, email/phone confirmation left unimplemented, role operations requiring the transactional service due to grantor tracking, account deletion blocked, and the AspNetCore framework reference added to support Identity types.

I'm reviewing the code I've written to catch any issues since I can't compile it directly. Let me check each file against the Dapper APIs and watch for common C# mistakes, starting with whether the Person class has the right structure for Dapper to map query results to it.

The Person class is sealed with a parameterless constructor and all properties have public setters, so Dapper can instantiate it and populate fields. The column aliases from the SQL query are all single words in PascalCase (like PersonIdentifier, Username, DisplayName), and Dapper matches these case-insensitively to the property names, so the mapping should work. I'm also verifying that the type mappings between C# and PostgreSQL are standard — Guid to uuid, DateTimeOffset to timestamptz, bool to bool, int to int, string to text, and byte[] to bytea — all of which Npgsql handles automatically.

Now I'm checking that the error handling constants and methods I'm using actually exist: PostgresErrorCodes.UniqueViolation and .CheckViolation are defined in the Npgsql namespace, PostgresException has a SqlState property, and IdentityErrorDescriber has the methods DuplicateUserName, InvalidUserName, and ConcurrencyFailure. I'm also verifying that the data protection extension methods Protect and Unprotect exist in the Microsoft.AspNetCore.DataProtection namespace.

For the cryptographic components, I'm confirming that Argon2id is available from Konscious.Security.Cryptography with the right constructor and methods, Stopwatch.GetElapsedTime exists in .NET 7+, and CryptographicOperations provides both FixedTimeEquals and ZeroMemory. I'm also verifying RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes and the IPasswordHasher<Person> interface are available.

Checking the namespace setup for the hasher — it's in MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity alongside Person, and it imports MyRestaurant.Domain.Security for the Argon2 utility types. The Argon2PhcString and Argon2Parameters classes from the Domain layer provide the encoding, parsing, and rehash-checking methods I need.

For the DI setup, I'm verifying that AddIdentityCore<Person> chains properly with AddUserStore<DapperUserStore> and AddDefaultTokenProviders, then replacing the password hasher with a singleton factory that returns Argon2idPasswordHasher as an IPasswordHasher<Person> implementation. swordHasher<Person> return. Lambda return type covariance: sp => new Argon2idPasswordHasher(...) targeting Func<IServiceProvider, IPasswordHasher<Person>> — the body expression type Argon2idPasswordHasher is implicitly convertible to IPasswordHasher

<Person>, so that checks out. Now I'm verifying the RestaurantMetrics class from the Observability namespace has the RecordPasswordHashDuration method that takes a double parameter for recording timing data.

The method group converts properly when passed as an Action<double> delegate. I'm also confirming that Argon2HashingOptions is accessible from the DataAccess.Identity namespace in the DI helper setup. Moving on to check Program.cs next.

In Program.cs, I've added the using statement for the Identity namespace and called AddRestaurantIdentity with the options parameter. Since the extension method returns IServiceCollection and we're just calling it as a statement without using the return value, that's fine. The top-level program structure with return codes still works correctly. For the test file, I'm using IAsyncLifetime from xUnit v3 to handle async initialization and cleanup with ValueTask methods. Verifying the remaining imports from the data access, domain, and identity layers are all available and properly namespaced. I'm checking whether the collection expression ["administrator", "kitchen"] can be target-typed to IEnumerable<string> for the Assert.Equal call. In C# 12, collection expressions do support interface types like IEnumerable<T>, IReadOnlyList<T>, and IList<T>, so this should work fine.

However, I'm concerned about overload resolution and type inference when a collection expression is passed as an argument — it can get tricky. To avoid any potential issues, I'll switch to using an explicit array with new[] syntax instead, which makes the type unambiguous. codes is an IReadOnlyList<string> from GenerateSet, which I can index into and pass as IEnumerable<string> to ReplaceCodesAsync. Now I need to reconsider the async exception assertions for the delete and role operations. Task is invoked inside the try block, so if it throws synchronously during the invocation itself, the exception gets caught by xUnit's ThrowsAsync handler. That should work correctly.

I could make the store methods async to return a faulted task for extra safety, but the synchronous throw pattern with => throw in a non-async method is actually a common and well-supported approach with ThrowsAsync, so I'll stick with it. The AddToRoleAsync call returns a Task, which matches the Func<Task> signature that ThrowsAsync expects.

For ReplaceCodesAsync, I'm using Dapper's multi-execute feature by passing a list of anonymous objects with the recovery code properties (TotpRecoveryCodeIdentifier, PersonIdentifier, CodeHash, CreatedAt) to ExecuteAsync, which will run the INSERT once per element and map the byte[] fields to bytea columns correctly.

Now I'm verifying the transaction handling — BeginTransactionAsync returns a ValueTask<DbTransaction>, and after awaiting it, I pass the DbTransaction to Dapper's ExecuteAsync via the CommandDefinition, which accepts IDbTransaction since DbTransaction implements that interface. Then I call CommitAsync on the transaction...

For RedeemCodeAsync, I'm executing an UPDATE statement with a subselect that returns the number of affected rows. The parameters are constructed from the current time via the clock, the user's PersonIdentifier, and the hashed code value.

Now looking at CountCodesAsync, I'm using ExecuteScalarAsync to get an integer count. The query uses SELECT count(*) which returns a bigint in Postgres, so I need to verify that Dapper and Npgsql properly handle the conversion from int8 down to Int32. first row as object, then converts it using Dapper's Parse<T> method. For numeric conversions like long to int, Dapper checks if the value is already the target type, and if not, uses Convert.ChangeType to handle the conversion — so a long count result would successfully convert to int rather than throwing an exception. Dapper's Convert.ChangeType safely converts long to int without throwing an exception, so ExecuteScalarAsync<int> works fine for count(*) results. Since the interface requires returning int anyway, I'll stick with this approach rather than adding extra casting or SQL type conversions. Now I'm verifying the return types across the remaining methods — GetUsersInRoleAsync returns a list of Person objects, GetRolesAsync returns a list of strings, and both CreateAsync and UpdateAsync properly return Task<IdentityResult> as the interface expects. I need to check what ToParameters returns to make sure it aligns with the method signature.

The ToParameters helper creates an anonymous object with all 14 properties that map directly to the SQL parameters, which Dapper uses to bind values correctly. For the AddIdentityCore configuration, I'm setting AllowedUserNameCharacters to an empty string to disable character validation, and explicitly setting RequiredUniqueChars to 1 (which is already the default).

Now I'm checking whether AddDefaultTokenProviders() causes any issues at startup — it registers the token providers but doesn't resolve them until they're actually needed, so there shouldn't be any eager dependency resolution problems when the app starts up. ataProtectorTokenProviderOptions` — it should be fine, it's standard.

Let me also double-check: does AddIdentityCore<Person> require Person to have specific members? No — it's generic over any class. The stores handle everything. ✓. But AddIdentityCore registers IUserClaimsPrincipalFactory<Person> = UserClaimsPrincipalFactory<Person>. That factory, when generating claims, uses UserManager.GetUserIdAsync, GetUserNameAsync, and (if SupportsUserRole) GetRolesAsync. It also reads Options.ClaimsIdentity claim types.

Actually, I should verify that AddIdentityCore<Person> doesn't require Person to implement any particular interface or have specific properties — it's completely generic, and the stores abstract away all the details. ✓ The claims principal factory will call standard UserManager methods, which work with any user type.

Now I'm checking whether AddRestaurantIdentity will cause dependency resolution issues at build time. In Development, ASP.NET Core enables scope and build validation by default, so the container will validate all registrations. The factory needs UserManager<Person> and IOptions<IdentityOptions>, both of which are registered, so that should be fine. Continuing through the dependency chain, Argon2idPasswordHasher constructs fine as a singleton since its factory just needs RestauterMetrics and creates a SemaphoreSlim with no side effects. UserManager<Person> pulls in DapperUserStore, IdentityOptions, the password hasher, and various validators—all resolvable during validation.

For token providers, DataProtectorTokenProvider<Person> needs IDataProtectionProvider, DataProtectorTokenProviderOptions, and a logger, which are all registered. The question is whether token providers get registered as concrete types in the DI container or only live in the provider map when AddDefaultTokenProviders calls AddTokenProvider with transient registration. All the token provider constructors are resolvable during ValidateOnBuild — AuthenticatorTokenProvider has no dependencies, the TOTP-based providers have parameterless constructors, and DataProtectorTokenProvider can resolve its IDataProtectionProvider, IOptions, and ILogger dependencies. Now checking IUserClaimsPrincipalFactory<Person>...

it depends on UserManager<Person> and IOptions<IdentityOptions>, both resolvable without hitting the database. So app.Build() should succeed in Development with ValidateOnBuild enabled. But there's a potential issue with ValidateScopes — I need to verify whether any singleton is depending on a scoped service, since Argon2idPasswordHasher is registered as a singleton and I need to confirm it only depends on options and actions, not scoped services.

The other singletons like RestaurantMetrics and IClock don't have scoped dependencies, which is good. Since I replaced the default scoped IPasswordHasher<Person> with a singleton, and UserManager<Person> (scoped) consumes it, that's fine — scoped consuming singleton isn't a captive dependency. But I should check if any other consumer might be expecting a different lifetime.

The Replace method should work correctly: it finds the first ServiceDescriptor matching IPasswordHasher<Person> (the one registered by AddIdentityCore as scoped) and removes it, then adds my singleton version. Since AddIdentityCore registers exactly one, I end up with a single singleton registration. The order is important though — I call AddIdentityCore first to register the scoped version, then Replace to swap it out for my singleton.

Now I'm checking whether ValidateOnBuild might fail when constructing dependencies. IUserConfirmation<Person> gets registered as DefaultUserConfirmation<Person> with a parameterless constructor, and ILookupNormalizer maps to UpperInvariantLookupNormalizer, also parameterless. Both should construct fine, so app.Build() should succeed.

One thing to verify: ValidateOnBuild skips open generics but validates closed ones, and all the Identity registrations are closed generics over Person, so they'll be checked. I've confirmed they're all constructible. Also, NpgsqlDatabaseConnectionFactory is registered as a singleton that creates a NpgsqlDataSource lazily without actually connecting to the database, so there's no DB hit at build time—this worked fine in the previous milestone too.

Now I'm ready to apply three targeted fixes: cast the count queries to ::int in both the store and test helper, and replace the collection expression in the test assertion with an array initializer.
